meta.title: Browser / VFS Control Plane
meta.description: A 10-turn, 50-agent CDO synthesis of browser host surfaces, WebMCP, AgentFS, ArchitectureIR, proof/eval fabric, and the proof-first implementation sequence for Leviathan.

props.title: Browser / VFS Control Plane
props.subtitle: The browser is real. The projected namespace is real. But the first implementation is proof-first, not filesystem-first.

props.content: We ran a 10-turn, 50-agent adaptive CDO on one question: what is the browser/WASM/VFS idea actually becoming in Leviathan? The run converged on a clean answer. Browser, mobile, extension, and WASM are host surfaces. AgentFS and projected filesystems are execution and projection substrates. WebMCP is a semantic page-tool adapter, not yet a namespace or scheduler. And the first thing to build is not a browser filesystem — it is a proof-first stack: explicit effect identity, fail-closed authority, receipt/finality, and only then the WebMCP A/B/C harness.

props.label: VERDICT
props.title: What the 50-agent run actually decided
props.subtitle: This is the shortest version of the answer before the details.

props.title: The core answer
props.content: Treat browser/WASM/VFS as host surfaces and execution/projection substrates, not as a new root runtime. The first spike is a proof-first WebMCP A/B/C harness after browser authority verification exists. Projected namespace work is deferred.

**Browser host surface:** an attended, capability-scoped control-plane client that can observe, present, approve, emit user actions, validate UI/runtime truth, and sometimes invoke bounded executors. **WebMCP:** a semantic page-tool adapter over `navigator.modelContext.tools` and `callTool()`, not yet a scheduler or namespace. **AgentFS:** a scoped mount projection plus writable overlay and execution substrate over canonical LevEvent, graph, proof, and receipt contracts. **ArchitectureIR:** classification and boundary refs only, never native runtime truth. **Proof/eval fabric:** the owner of proof obligations, witness sets, receipt policy, ratchet admission, and settlement eligibility.

props.label: BOUNDARY STACK
props.title: The concrete architecture
props.subtitle: Six layers, each with one job.

props.caption: Boundary stack
props.content:
flowchart TD
  A[AdmissionEffectEnvelope v0
core/domain/src/poly-protocol/admission-effect.ts] --> B[Browser authority profile
AgentPing owns live browser authority]
  A --> C[AgentFS authority/finality profile
transaction-owned guarded storage]
  A --> D[Proof/eval policy profile
receipt policy + settlement eligibility]
  A --> E[Gateway profile
transport, trace, fallback equivalence]
  A --> F[Projection / namespace layer
scoped resources and views only]

rows.responsibility: effect_id, authority_ref, proof_spec_ref, receipt_policy_ref, owner-owned profiles
rows.must_not: global policy substrate with every optional field

rows.responsibility: caller token, active lease equality, scope and target checks before side effects
guarded capability + same-transaction mutation and final receipt
rows.must_not: raw production handles or post-hoc receipts

rows.responsibility: receipt policy enforcement, witness sets, ratchet admission, settlement eligibility
rows.must_not: live browser authority or native runtime semantics

rows.responsibility: transport equivalence, trace and receipt propagation, fallback attestation
rows.must_not: business semantics or native browser/VFS truth

rows.responsibility: scoped resources and views over refs and receipts
rows.must_not: graph truth, policy truth, or settlement truth

props.label: WEBMCP
props.title: What WebMCP changes — and what it does not
props.subtitle: The run was brutally clear about this.

props.title: ✅ What it is
props.content: A semantic page-tool adapter. Structured eyes and hands over browser-native capabilities. Cheap, local, and useful for bounded A/B/C falsification.

props.title: ⛔ What it is not
props.content: Not a scheduler. Not a namespace. Not a market. Not a filesystem. Not a durable authority layer. If it just renames `callTool()` as file paths, it is redundant.

props.label: ROLE MAP
props.title: Who does what
props.subtitle: The run converged on a clean role split.

props.title: Client
props.content: Issues intent/effect requests, presents authority, receives projections and receipts, and never gets ambient executor handles.

props.title: Gateway
props.content: Owns transport bridge lifecycle, fallback equivalence, trace and receipt propagation. No native business semantics.

props.title: Worker
props.content: Executes bounded effects under an admitted envelope and emits effect results and receipts. Does not self-authorize.

props.title: Provider
props.content: Supplies native capability: CDP, WebMCP, AgentFS, model, tool, or remote service behind a contract.

props.title: Verifier
props.content: Evaluates proof and receipts, attests or rejects eligibility, but does not execute the effect and does not own native semantics.

props.label: TENSIONS
props.title: The live tensions that survived 10 turns
props.subtitle: These are why the implementation is proof-first.

rows.risk: Convenience paths become invisible authority bypasses
rows.resolved: Authority must block side effects before CDP/tool/DOM/router/auth paths run

rows.risk: Projected paths get mistaken for graph or policy truth
rows.resolved: Namespace is a scoped view only; it carries refs and evidence, not ontology

rows.risk: One type swallows browser, market, settlement, gateway, and proof semantics
rows.resolved: One base envelope with owner-owned profiles

rows.tension: Receipt identity vs false green settlement
rows.risk: Quorum or naked IDs release settlement without real effect identity
Raw storage can commit before final receipt exists
rows.resolved: Same-transaction guarded finality or no claim of finality

props.label: FIRST SLICE
props.title: The proof-first implementation sequence
props.subtitle: This is the real output of the run.

props.title: 1. Contract wave
props.content: Create `core/domain/src/poly-protocol/`, add only `./poly-protocol` export, implement `AdmissionEffectEnvelope v0`, add resolver probes, root-import negatives, owner/profile tests.

props.title: 2. Receipt identity wave
props.content: Add `effectId` / `effectIds`, preserve `proofSpecId` and `verifierResultIds`, and enforce settlement-hard against quorum-only bypasses and naked IDs.

props.title: 3. Browser authority wave
props.content: Add AgentPing browser authority verifier and wire `/api/v1/tool` and `/api/v1/cdp` before any browser-cascade or WebMCP promotion.

props.title: 4. AgentFS finality wave
props.content: Add guarded-finality conformance tests and raw-handle compile-fail tests before changing storage behavior.

props.title: 5. WebMCP falsification wave
props.content: Add the local A/B/C harness only as a negative test surface. No namespace promotion until upstream verifier and shared `effect_id` are proven.

props.label: DEFERRED
props.title: What the run explicitly pushed out
props.subtitle: This matters because the temptation was to jump straight into productizing the browser filesystem idea.

props.content: Deferred by the run: projected WebMCP/browser namespace, OPFS, `/browser/...` trees, generator/registry/marketplace work, selector healing, live ChatGPT canaries, full AgentFS POSIX parity, checksum/scrub, multiwriter overlay policy, Turso remote sync finality, x402 settlement execution, remote worker market overlays, reputation, and auth-sniffer redesign. The browser/VFS idea was not rejected. It was demoted from first spike to later wave until proof, authority, and receipt semantics are real.

props.label: NEXT
props.title: If you build one thing next, build this
props.subtitle: The browser filesystem dream survives only if the substrate underneath is mechanically trustworthy.

props.title: Build proof, then projection
props.content: Do not start with namespace streaming, browser filesystem metaphors, or market overlays. Start with `AdmissionEffectEnvelope v0`, explicit `effect_id`, fail-closed browser authority, guarded AgentFS finality, and the local WebMCP A/B/C falsifier.

props.html: Leviathan HomeWebMCP VisionConstraint Engineering
